title: Bundle-Dyeing Old Clothes
content:
The author stopped purchasing new clothes in 2015 due to concerns about the fashion industry's social and environmental costs. She now helps others upcycle their garments through natural dyeing, offering in-studio workshops in Berlin and remote services for those elsewhere.

The article outlines key requirements for plant dyeing: natural fibers only (cotton, linen, wool, silk), pre-washing with appropriate methods, and understanding that "plant dyes are translucent, whatever color is used as a 'base', it's going to be visible after dyeing."

She explains the preparation process using aluminium formate as a pre-fixative, which she soaks fibers in for at least 24 hours. For the featured turtleneck project, she used only dried rose petals, applying them minimally to create subtle beige splatters that would disguise existing and future stains.

The bundle-dyeing technique involves scattering plant material on fabric, rolling it tightly, wrapping it in cloth, then exposing it to heat through steaming or simmering. Steaming creates clearer prints while simmering allows dye to transfer more broadly.

The finished turtleneck displays multi-colored speckles ranging from brown to purple despite using a single dye source. She emphasizes that "plant color durability is a spectrum" and expects the colors to gradually shift over time.

title: 16 Colors with Just 3 Plant Dyes
content:
The author describes a plant dyeing workshop exploring how to create numerous color variations from just three dye plants.

Using Different Fibers: Plant dyes produce varying results depending on fiber type. "Wool and silk take up the color much better than cotton, and linen usually shields the palest results." The author demonstrates this with chamomile-dyed cotton samples showing different depths of yellow based on fabric weight and weave.

Modifying with Mordants: Metal salts enhance dye longevity and alter final colors. Aluminum intensifies vibrancy while iron "saddens" or mutes colors containing tannins. The author shows hollyhock samples modified with iron for subtly different shades.

Changing pH Levels: "Low pH (acidic solution) pushes the colors towards the warmer end of the spectrum, and high pH (alkaline solution) pushes them towards the colder end." Madder-dyed samples treated with vinegar versus baking soda demonstrate shifting coral reds to orange or deeper red.

Over-dyeing: Sequential dyeing with different plants allows better color control than mixing dye baths simultaneously. The sequence matters—hollyhock over-dyed with chamomile produces different greens than chamomile over-dyed with hollyhock.

The author invites workshop participation and offers ebooks on natural dyeing basics and mordants.

title: Printing with Plant Dyes
content:
This tutorial demonstrates how to create printed textiles using plant dyes through a multi-step process. The author printed bedsheets using an iron mordant paste applied via squeegee stamping, creating rusty-beige marks that become dark when dyed with tannin-rich plants.

The process involves: (1) Making ferrous acetate paste by combining iron sulfate, white vinegar, and soda ash, then thickening with gum arabic or guar gum; (2) Applying the paste to dry fabric using blocks, brushes, or screens; (3) Rinsing thoroughly and neutralizing with a chalk and wheat bran solution to remove residual gum; (4) Dyeing with concentrated tara powder or other "tannin-rich dyes like oak galls, sumac, tannic acid, black tea, cutch, etc." where the iron-mordanted areas turn dark while unmordanted fabric remains uncolored.

The author emphasizes this is challenging for beginners due to ingredient quantities and container size requirements. The bedsheet project required 8 hours of printing over 2 days, with additional drying periods between steps. Key supplies needed include iron acetate, gum thickener, chalk, wheat bran, scoured natural-fiber textiles, and dedicated non-food utensils.

title: Upcycling Clothes with Natural Dyes
content:
The article presents a comprehensive tutorial on upcycling second-hand clothing using natural dyes and iron mordants. The author emphasizes that "there is no better way to care for the planet than using what we already have," noting that upcycling textiles has minimal environmental impact compared to new eco-fashion alternatives.

The process requires three essential items: rusty nails or steel wool, white vinegar, and a tannin-rich dye source such as black tea, acorns, or avocado stones. The tutorial guides readers through creating iron acetate mordant by steeping steel wool in vinegar for approximately two weeks, then proceeding through steps including fiber preparation, scrunching fabric with rubber bands to create resist patterns, mordanting, dye extraction, dyeing, and thorough rinsing.

The author demonstrates the technique on a 100% cotton sweatshirt found at a Berlin textile market, resulting in distinctive rusty-colored patterns on a white background. The article emphasizes working with available materials as a creative constraint and documents the upcycling of multiple second-hand garments throughout the project.

title: Turning Natural Dye into Pigment
content:
The article explains the process of converting leftover plant dye baths into usable pigments. It begins by distinguishing between dyes (water-soluble substances) and pigments (insoluble particles that disperse rather than dissolve).

The tutorial outlines a step-by-step procedure: strain the dye bath through fine muslin, dissolve potassium aluminum sulfate (alum) at approximately 10g per liter to bind dye particles, then neutralize the acidic mixture using soda ash (about 5g per liter). The pH should reach neutral (7). After pigments settle, remove the clear water using a rubber pear, then filter the remaining liquid through coffee filters. Once dry, grind the pigment chunks in a mortar and store in glass jars.

Key advantages of pigments over dye extracts include easier handling, simpler filtering, better dispersal in painting mediums, and improved lightfastness due to aluminum's mordanting properties. The author notes that lightfastness varies depending on the original plant material used.

title: Eco-Printing with Leaves
content:
Eco-printing transfers plant colors onto fabric by pressing the entire dye plant flat against cloth. Unlike bundle dyeing which uses scattered plant pieces, eco-printing requires keeping the plant image flat for complete surface contact. The process involves laying mordanted cloth, arranging plants on top, optionally adding a plastic barrier, rolling everything tightly around a rod, securing with string, and steaming.

The author describes numerous variations: different mordants (aluminum, aluminum+tannins, aluminum+iron), fabric moisture levels (wet or dry), leaf preparation (dry, water-soaked, or iron-water-soaked), and techniques like using an iron blanket to create negative images.

Key findings from testing include: water-soaked leaves produce clearer prints with reduced halos; iron-water-soaked leaves on aluminum create very dark prints; dry cloth with soaked leaves yields the sharpest results; leaf undersides (vein side) print better than top sides; and an iron blanket creates striking resist effects. The author emphasizes that "at least one of the elements needs to be wet" for effective dye transfer.

The piece recommends collecting fallen leaves in autumn, pressing them between book pages for later use, and experimenting with various plant species. Maple leaves are highlighted for their intricate shapes and dyeing potential.

title: Oak Leaves Dyeing Tutorial
content:
This guide teaches natural dyeing using oak leaves to create warm grey tones. The tutorial begins by explaining that autumn provides abundant plant-dyeing materials despite seeming less vibrant than summer.

The process requires oak leaves, natural fibers (cotton, linen, wool, or silk), iron crystals, and dedicated dyeing equipment. The author collected leaves from the ground on an autumn day, noting that "this dye material is quite potent so you don't need much."

To extract color, leaves are simmered in approximately five liters of water for over an hour. Fibers are pre-soaked in warm water to ensure even dyeing. The author skipped traditional mordanting because oak's tannins bond well with natural fibers, particularly when iron will be applied afterward.

The strained dye bath receives soaked fibers for about an hour. Rather than heating continuously, the author uses the dye's residual warmth, allowing fibers to absorb color through extended soaking. Stirring frequently prevents uneven coloring.

Iron crystals dissolved in hot water create a modifier bath that shifts the beige tone toward grey. For darker results, the process repeats: return fibers to the dye bath, apply iron modification, and steam them over boiling water to deepen the shade through tannin oxidation.

The resulting color proves lightfast and wash-fast due to iron's binding properties, though acidic substances can discharge the dye.

title: Dyeing with Beetroot
content:
The article explains why beetroot and similar food items are unsuitable for dyeing fabric and yarn that needs lasting color. The author emphasizes that "foodstuff is not dyestuff" because these materials produce fugitive dyes that fade quickly, even when hidden from sunlight.

The piece stresses the importance of mordanting fibers before dyeing, explaining that mordants act as a link between fiber and dye particles. Aluminum formate is recommended as the preferred mordant.

The author then provides alternatives to food-based dyes that offer reliable, lightfast colors: madder root (reds and pinks), brazilwood (purples and pinks), cochineal (oranges to burgundy), avocado (soft pinks), logwood (dark purples), and indigo (true blue). Each option includes application notes and color-shifting information.

The article concludes with care instructions for naturally-dyed pieces, recommending hand washing in lukewarm water with wool soap and avoiding direct sunlight exposure.

title: How to Dye with Plants
content:
Plant dyeing has become increasingly popular online, but the craft requires practice and knowledge to master properly. The author shares foundational concepts for natural dyeing after years of experience since 2015.

The basic process follows five steps: "SCOUR — MORDANT — RINSE — DYE — RINSE"

Only natural fibers work with plant dyes. "Fibers coming from animals (silk, wool, etc) take up the colors better than the fibers coming from plants (cotton, linen, hemp)."

Traditional dyes proven effective over centuries include weld, madder, indigo, woad, logwood, fustic, osage, and oak galls. The author also experiments with locally foraged plants like marigolds, goldenrod, and walnut husks, though these may lack the lightfastness of traditional options.

Fiber preparation involves three stages: scouring removes protective coatings, mordanting applies metal salts to create chemical bonds with dye molecules, and rinsing removes excess mordant. "Scour your fibers by cooking them for 1h (temperature and substances according to the fiber type) and then mordant the fibers with metal salt."

Dye extraction requires dissolving plant matter in water at varying temperatures depending on material. Harder materials like acorns need extended boiling, while delicate flowers require minimal heat. The mordanted fibers then simmer in the strained dye bath for at least an hour with regular stirring.

The article includes a beginner exercise using onion peels, avocado stones, and coffee to dye small wool samples over two days.

title: List of Natural Mordants
content:
This article explores natural mordants used in plant dyeing to improve color longevity. The author clarifies that "chemical and natural are not mutually exclusive," noting that traditional mordants like aluminum, iron, and copper are both chemical substances and naturally occurring.

Mordants are defined as substances forming insoluble connections between fibers and dyes. Aluminum salts are the most commonly used, with potassium aluminum sulphate (alum) working on animal fibers, aluminum acetate on cellulose and silk, and aluminum formate working universally across fiber types.

Iron salts function as both mordants and modifiers, darkening many dyes. Iron sulfate works best on plant fibers, while iron acetate suits wool and silk but requires home preparation.

The article distinguishes mordants from binders like soy milk and tannins, which form soluble rather than permanent bonds. Substances like salt, vinegar, and baking soda "DO NOT have any influence on the color stability or intensity."

Alternative processes include indigo dyeing (which uses oxidation) and acid dyeing (combining dyes, tannins, and acids without mordants). Practical examples show onion skins dyed with different mordants producing olive green, yellow-orange, and vibrant orange results.

title: Tips for Natural Dyeing Clothes with Plants
content:
The article discusses practical considerations for plant dyeing in fashion. The author, an artisan dyer, explains that plant dyeing works best for limited editions rather than large-scale production due to time, water usage, and sustainability concerns.

Fabric Requirements: Only natural fibers like wool, silk, cotton, and linen bond with plant dyes. Fabrics must be thoroughly washed at 90°C and pre-treated with mordants before dyeing.

Plant Selection: Traditional dye plants like madder root and weld produce colorfast results, while kitchen waste and florist flowers typically fade quickly. Local collection is sustainable for small projects only.

Colorfast Solutions: Metal salt mordants (iron and aluminum) are essential for lightfast, vibrant colors. "Plant dyes are not pH-stain resistant" and require hand washing in neutral detergent and shade drying.

Design Considerations: Natural dyes are transparent and produce uneven results. Testing multiple fabric samples (minimum 15x15cm) is necessary since colors vary by fabric type. Bundle dyeing and shibori techniques help embrace natural variations.

Process Requirements: Projects demand extensive planning including mordanting, testing, and evaluation. The author recommends allowing several months rather than one month for proper execution.

title: Simple Patterns on a Frame Loom - Weaving Twill, Chevron, Diamond
content:
This comprehensive tutorial teaches frame loom weavers how to interpret floor loom weaving patterns. The author explains that "every vertical row of boxes corresponds to a single warp thread, and every horizontal row of boxes corresponds to a single weft thread."

The guide covers four main patterns:

1. Houndstooth Pattern: Uses 24 warp ends (5 repeats of 4 ends, plus 2 extra on each side). The sequence involves alternating between white and black yarn in 4-pass blocks, creating distinctive diagonal effects through over-and-under combinations.

2. Straight Twill: Creates diagonal visual lines using a straightforward repeating sequence of over-2, under-2 motions.

3. Chevron (Reverse Twill): Described as "a very elegant yet simple" pattern that reverses direction after the initial four steps, producing V-shaped formations.

4. Diamond Twill: Uses a 6-end pattern repeat, creating a lovely geometric pattern through specific over-and-under sequences.

The tutorial includes practical advice about yarn selection, warping techniques, and using shed sticks as heddles. The author suggests these techniques work equally well for making wall hangings, patches, or samples.

title: Patchworking Tutorial - DIY Quilted Bag
content:
This comprehensive guide explains how to create a quilted bag from fabric scraps. The author uses plant-dyed cotton canvas offcuts to minimize waste while producing unique, colorful patchwork pieces.

The tutorial covers essential steps: gathering materials (fabric scraps, sewing machine, iron, cutting tools), trimming scraps with parallel edges, and arranging patches thoughtfully to ensure color balance and structural integrity. The author emphasizes "never put more than three edges next to each other" when working with thick fabrics to avoid bulkiness.

The core technique involves creating small blocks by sewing scraps together one piece at a time, pressing seams flat after each addition, and trimming regularly. These blocks are then connected in a planned sequence. The author provides troubleshooting advice and demonstrates cutting the final piece to size using a pattern.

The finished bag features organic cotton canvas, unbleached linen lining, hemp webbing handles, and flax string drawstrings—entirely vegan, natural, and zero-waste.

title: Plant Dye Modifiers: Paint Patterns with Iron Water and Vinegar
content:
The article presents a comprehensive tutorial on creating patterns on plant-dyed fabric using iron water and vinegar solutions. The author, who works professionally with plant dyes, shares techniques learned from observing students in a dyeing workshop.

The guide covers making iron water by collecting rusty materials and soaking them in vinegar for at least two weeks, or using iron crystals at a 1-2% concentration. White vinegar serves as a discharge agent to create negative space patterns by breaking the bond between dye, fiber, and metal.

Key sections address selecting appropriate dye plants—specifically tannin-rich varieties like oak galls, alder cones, and tea that react with iron modifiers. The tutorial emphasizes differences between lightweight and heavyweight fabrics: lightweight fabrics absorb iron water quickly requiring careful application, while heavier fabrics like canvas need multiple strokes but allow more precise work.

The author recommends working methodically, allowing time for color reactions to develop rather than rushing the process. Pattern possibilities range from geometric designs to polka dots created with sponge brushes. The article concludes by discussing how acidic solutions can discharge iron patterns and suggests experimenting with pH-sensitive dyes to observe color shifts.

title: How to Warp a Frame Loom with a Heddle Bar
content:
This tutorial covers the fundamentals of warping a frame loom with a heddle bar. The author explains that "Warp are all vertical threads you stretch between top and bottom beam of the loom."

The process involves several key steps:

1. Preparation: You'll need a loom with adjustable beams, a heddle bar, and non-stretchy thread like cotton or linen.

2. Attachment: Create a loop and secure the thread to a tooth on the top beam with a tight knot.

3. Threading: Pull the thread through the heddle bar grooves, wrapping it around corresponding bottom beam teeth, then back through the next groove.

4. Pattern: Each thread passes once through each heddle bar groove but twice through each warping beam groove.

5. Completion: Continue back-and-forth threading, then secure with knots at the bottom beam.

6. Tension Adjustment: The loom allows post-warping tension modifications via adjustable nodes.

7. Weaving: The heddle bar creates alternating shed openings, allowing you to pass the weft shuttle between warp threads.

The guide emphasizes: "It's important that your warp is stretched properly and has a good tension" to achieve neat results.

title: Dyeing with Indigo and Fresh Woad
content:
The author describes attending a 3-day workshop in Harz, Germany to learn indigo vat-making and fresh woad dyeing techniques. She explains that indigo pigment requires chemical reduction to become soluble in water, creating an oxygen-free dye bath called a vat.

The workshop covered two main vat types: a non-eco-friendly synthetic indigo vat using ammonia and hydrosulfide, which produced "very clear, pure navy blue" results, and an organic vat using lime and fructose that required two days to reduce but proved environmentally safer.

The author then experimented with fresh woad leaves, which contain isatin rather than indigotin. After cooking and aerating the leaves, she applied the same vat recipes, noting the results appeared "more alive and natural" with subtle, greyish tones compared to synthetic indigo.

She subsequently tested fresh leaf dye baths at home, blending refrigerated woad leaves with cold water. This produced beautiful colors—pale blue wool and turquoise silk—though the bath's effectiveness diminished quickly. She plans to test light fastness and continue growing woad for future experiments.

The author emphasizes knowledge-sharing within the natural dyeing community and mentions publishing ebooks on natural dyeing basics and mordants available on Etsy.

title: 5 Tips for Dyeing Pink with Avocado
content:
Avocado is described as a favorite dye source that produces colors "from soft peach to deep coral pink, if you treat them right." The author shares five techniques for achieving vibrant results:

1. Use black skins only - "Avocados with black skins make the nicest pinks. Green skins don't have much pink pigment in them." Stones from both types work, and materials from just three avocados can dye a t-shirt.

2. Freeze both skins and stones - Freezing preserves potency better than drying. Red spots appearing on black skins indicate readiness for the dye pot. Prolonged storage causes color to become "muddy" and less brilliant.

3. Give it time - The extraction process involves multiple heating cycles over several days, with the author repeating extraction "even up to 5 times before all pigment is extracted." This allows pigment oxidation, deepening the final color.

4. Shift the pH - Alkaline conditions (using baking soda or lime) produce deeper pinks, while vinegar shifts toward orange tones. This technique can revive stored dye baths.

5. Steam for a deeper color - Steaming fibers over hot dye baths causes additional oxidation, darkening the final result.

The author notes that avocados work well with both protein and cellulose fibers and mentions using soya milk or no pretreatment for mordanting.

title: How to Weave a Rag Rug on a Frame Loom
content:
The article provides a comprehensive guide to weaving a rag rug on a frame loom. The author created this project for bathroom use, designed to absorb water from wet feet while adding a handcrafted aesthetic.

Materials needed include "a loom with detachable warp bars, strong cotton or linen warp thread, a few pieces of cardboard for warping and leftover fabric strips." The author dyed old linen with alder cones and iron, then cut it into strips measuring 0.7cm to 1.5cm wide.

The process involves several key steps: securing the base with cardboard and cotton thread passes, weaving with fabric strips using an over-under pattern, connecting strips using a technique involving small eyelets, and maintaining even warp spacing throughout. The author emphasizes beating the fabric down firmly to create structure.

Once initial sections are complete, weavers advance the project by rewinding onto lower bars, continuing until reaching desired length. Finishing involves cutting warp threads in groups of two, tying overhand knots, and unwinding the completed rug.

The final product requires minimal finishing, with options for keeping fringe or adding fabric tape to edges. The natural linen construction provides moisture absorption and fast drying capabilities.

title: My Local Dye Plants to Color Fabric and Yarn
content:
The article explores extracting natural dyes from locally available plants rather than relying solely on traditional sources. The author describes adopting a practice-based approach, experimenting with plants collected during daily walks in Berlin, Germany.

The guide emphasizes collecting plants respectfully, taking only what's needed while preserving plant survival and wildlife food sources. Storage methods vary by plant type—most dried plants retain dye potential when stored in breathable paper bags, though some plants like goldenrod work better when frozen to maintain color vibrancy.

The article acknowledges that certain dyes lack lightfastness and will fade regardless of mordants used. These remain valuable for hobby projects and seasonal redyeing.

The comprehensive plant directory organizes dyes by color categories: yellows from chamomile and goldenrod; greens from nettles and ferns; oranges and pinks from willow leaves and firethorn berries; purples and blues from mahonia and elderberries; and browns and greys from oak and walnut materials. Kitchen waste alternatives include turmeric, onion skins, and avocado pits, though these typically lack permanence.

The author recommends growing traditional dye plants like woad, madder root, and weld to achieve a full color spectrum through overdyeing techniques.

title: How to Weave a Scarf on a Frame Loom
content:
The tutorial explains how to create longer woven pieces using special frame looms with detachable warping beams and metal clamps. The author offers three loom sizes: small (7.5 inches), medium (12 inches), and large (20 inches).

Essential tools include the loom, metal clamps, cardboard strips, warp and weft yarns, and "a long table or another long and sturdy piece of wood, that you can secure your loom to."

The process involves detaching and repositioning the top beam, securing the loom with clamps, then attaching the warp threads between the two beams. The author recommends using "softer yarns like wool" for scarves rather than standard cotton or linen.

A critical step involves "wind the beam under the warp threads" while inserting cardboard separators to prevent tension problems. After completing the initial weaving section, users gradually release more warp by turning the bottom beam to continue the project.

The author emphasizes weaving "rather loosely otherwise it will become too stiff" and provides references to additional detailed weaving instructions on their blog.

title: Plant Dyeing Directory - Acid Dyes, Metal Mordants or Soy Milk
content:
The article presents a comprehensive flowchart-based guide to plant dyeing techniques. It begins by acknowledging the complexity of botanical dyeing and the abundance of sometimes conflicting information available online.

The guide directs readers through a series of decision questions based on their dyeing purpose: business (requiring colorfast products), personal projects, or healing properties. Depending on answers regarding mordant preference and fiber type (cellulose or protein), users are directed to six distinct techniques.

These techniques include: direct dyes (requiring no mordants), acid dyes (using vinegar or citric acid for protein fibers), metal mordants for cellulose fibers (aluminum, iron, copper, tin), metal mordants for protein fibers, soy milk pretreatment (described as "a binder" rather than a mordant), and skipping pretreatment for casual protein fiber dyeing.

The author emphasizes that "mordants won't help if the dyes you use are fugitive," recommending traditional dyes like madder, weld, and cochineal over common but unreliable options like red cabbage or beetroot.

Safety guidance stresses using gloves, protective clothing, face masks, and dedicated tools when working with metal mordants. The article concludes by encouraging readers to conduct personal color fastness tests, as results depend on individual circumstances including water quality and dye stuff quality.

title: Bundle Dyeing Tutorial - Silk Scarf Dyed with Flowers
content:
This comprehensive guide teaches bundle dyeing, a technique that creates unique patterns by arranging flowers on fabric before steaming. The tutorial covers everything from preparation through final finishing.

The process begins with gathering dried flowers like hollyhock, hibiscus, chamomile, and marigolds. The fabric should be pre-mordanted silk or natural material for best results. Supplies needed include pH modifiers (citric acid and baking soda), string, and basic kitchen equipment like a pot and cheesecloth.

The dyeing method involves scattering flowers on half the fabric, folding to contain them, rolling tightly, and wrapping with string to prevent movement. The bundle then steams for 1-1.5 hours, with rotation halfway through for even coloring. After cooling overnight, the flowers are removed and the scarf is rinsed thoroughly.

Color modification occurs through pH adjustment: citric acid produces pink tones, while baking soda creates blue-green hues. The final steps include air drying and careful removal of any remaining plant matter. Future washing requires maintaining consistent pH levels using the same modifiers employed during the final color adjustment.

title: How to Weave on a Simple Loom
content:
The article provides a comprehensive guide to creating weavings using a DIY cardboard loom. The author explains that "All you need is a piece of cardboard (A4 size), a cutter, scissors, a ruler and a pencil, and a wool needle."

The tutorial covers constructing a loom by drawing evenly-spaced lines on cardboard, cutting a central window for visibility, and creating small notches for securing threads. The author recommends using non-stretchy yarn for the warp (vertical threads) and decorative yarn for the weft (horizontal threads).

Key weaving techniques include alternating over-and-under patterns with each row, creating "bubbles" in thread to avoid over-tensioning, and changing colors by overlapping threads on existing warp threads. The guide emphasizes that "If your tension your weft while weaving, it will distort your warp AND your weaving will get wavy when taken off the loom."

Finishing involves hemstitching the ends by looping over pairs of warp threads, weaving in loose ends carefully, and tying off warp threads before removal. The author notes that finished narrow weavings can become "bracelets, bookmarks, ornaments, pacifier holders, etc."

The tutorial concludes by recommending wooden looms with adjustable tension for larger projects.

title: How to Print with Plants onto Fabric
content:
You don't need complicated tools or special skills to print fabric. All you need is paint, a small sponge, and an object you want to print. Each plant has its unique structure, shape, and size. This tutorial shows how to print with leaves but you can also use twigs, berries, or flowers.

Collect plants: Pick any plant with interesting shapes. Flat plants like leaves work best, though berries or twigs can work too. Choose sturdy plants that won't be too fragile during the printing process. Collect multiple pieces in different sizes.

Choose the layout: Clean your plants and prepare your work surface. Protect your table with newspaper if printing larger cloth. Play with different patterns—geometrical or random layouts. Take a photo of your chosen pattern for reference.

Paint the leaves: Use textile paint (water-based works well). Apply paint onto plants using a small sponge stamp. "The pattern will look better if you print with the underside of the leaves. The underside has visible veins which will make your print more detailed."

Print leaves onto fabric: Lay your leaf on fabric and cover with thick paper. Apply gentle but firm pressure—too little creates faint patterns, too much flattens texture. Peel up leaves carefully without sliding them sideways. Practice on scrap cloth first.

Iron your print: Let fabric dry following paint instructions. Iron to make prints waterproof and set the paint. Iron after each layer when using multiple colors.
